column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Cobalt Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/metal/specialty/cobalt-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Cobalt oxide removal without substrate damage
Near-IR wavelength for optimal Cobalt absorption and thermal processing
Optimal beam diameter for precision cleaning of Cobalt components
High repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage on Cobalt surfaces
Fluence threshold for Cobalt oxide removal without substrate damage
Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient Cobalt oxide ablation
Scanning speed for efficient Cobalt surface cleaning
Number of cleaning passes for complete Cobalt oxide removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform Cobalt surface cleaning

Typical range for industrial cleaning of reflective metals like cobalt to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100 W balances efficiency and safety. Max limited to prevent melting (cobalt MP ~1495°C). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs for metal cleaning.

Effective range for contamination removal; slower for heavy contaminants (e.g., oxides), faster for light; 1500 mm/s optimal for uniform cleaning without overheating. Source: CleanLaser equipment manuals and industry standards for 1064 nm cleaning.

Nanosecond regime typical for industrial cleaning to ablate contaminants while minimizing HAZ on high-conductivity cobalt; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-low heat but less common industrially. 20 ns recommended for balance. Source: Laser physics studies in Optics & Laser Technology (e.g., ns vs ps ablation thresholds).

Standard for pulsed cleaning; higher rates speed up process but risk heat accumulation on reflective cobalt; 50 kHz optimal for controlled energy delivery. Trade-off: high freq increases throughput but requires monitoring temperature. Source: Academic literature in Applied Surface Science on metal cleaning parameters.

Fluence range for safe contaminant ablation; cobalt damage threshold ~2-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to reflectivity (~60%); 1.5 J/cm² recommended for cleaning without substrate harm. Conservative for cobalt's properties. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (similar to Ni/Co alloys).

Beam diameter range for precision vs area coverage; smaller for fine surface prep on cobalt, larger for broad cleaning; 200 μm recommended for effective fluence distribution. Source: Laser equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf for industrial applications.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy; 2 passes typical for complete removal on cobalt without excessive heat buildup. Source: Industry case studies in laser cleaning of aerospace alloys (e.g., similar to Co-based superalloys).

Ensures uniform coverage; 60% recommended for even cleaning on metallic surfaces like cobalt, avoiding gaps or overexposure. Range balances efficiency and homogeneity. Source: Process optimization in Surface & Coatings Technology journals.
